taskVersion.name: epu_q2_long
taskVersion.definition:
@kbench.task(name="epu_q2_long")
def epu_q2_long(
    llm,
    article_key: str,
    content_for_eval: str,
    gold_epu: int,
    short_pre: str = "",
    short_post: str = "",
    medium_pre: str = "",
    medium_post: str = "",
    long_pre: str = "",
    long_post: str = "",
    disagreement_flag: int = 0,
) -> float:
    out = model_json_call(llm, build_q2_prompt(content_for_eval, long_pre, long_post))
    m = binary_match(out["epu"], gold_epu)
    return 0.0 if m is None else float(m)

parts.text:
You are evaluating only the text between TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END.

Task:
Decide whether the TARGET ARTICLE expresses ECONOMIC POLICY UNCERTAINTY (EPU).

Labeling rule:
- EPU = 1 only if the TARGET ARTICLE discusses uncertainty about government policy,
  policy decisions, possible policy actions, political outcomes affecting policy,
  or the economic effects of policy.
- EPU = 0 for general economics, elections, markets, war, uncertainty, or foreign events
  unless the uncertainty is specifically tied to economic policy.

IMPORTANT:
- Use only the text inside TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END for the decision.
- Return ONLY valid JSON.
- No markdown. No explanation. No code fence.

Return exactly these keys:
{
  "epu": 0 or 1,
  "confidence": number between 0 and 1,
  "focus_excerpt": "short quote from target article only"
}

TARGET ARTICLE START
Last of three partsLOS ANGELES -- Ray Ramos is a man of faith in a city in desperate need of hope.He is pastor of the Hispanic congregation at a unique chareh in a neighborhtod where the world intersects west of downtown.His congregants make up the fastest-growing segment of Los Angeles' exploding immigrant population -- the refugees of war and famine from Central America.They are America's modern ptor and huddled masses.Each week, Mr. Ramos and a few of his congregants wander the teeming, low-rise neighborhtods near drug-infested MacArthur Park to invite the new arrivals to Wilshire Christian Chareh (Disciples of Christ).A magnificent monument built in 1925 at Wilshire Boulevard and Normandie Avenue, the chareh for decades served a predominantly white congregation back when Wilshire was the Champs Elysees of Los Angeles.But like much of inner-city Los Angeles during the past 20 years, the neighborhtod changed. isnlike much of inner-city Los Angeles, the white congregation didn't bail out.Instead, the mostly white chareh opened its vast wtoden dtors to its new neighbors -- Koreans, Hispanics and Filipinos.Today, Wilshire Christian Chareh is home to four congregations led by four ministers speaking four languages, all spreading one message -- love and understanding -- to a city unlike any other in the world.Monthly, the four cultures gather for a unity service -- a celebration of shared hope in a city divided by difference: E pluribus unum. "Out of many, one.'It is the challen

[...TRUNCATED...]

roving rioters a year ago, he said."Around here, there is still a lot of stress in the air,' he said, pausing as he concluded a tour of the chareh, founded in 1874 as a mission of the First Christian Chareh at 11th Street and Hope.Reaching out to newly arrived Americans, like Eric from Peru, is one way to alleviate tension, he said. By inviting immigrants to unity services with other immigrants and American-born chareh members, walls of misunderstanding are shattered."It is going to take a lot of work, a lot of love and understanding to turn L.A. around,' said Mr. Ramos."That is why it is important for all of us to be here together,' he said. The chareh's mammoth sanctuary is still, lit softly by sunlight through rose-colored windows."You see, in here we're all equal.'Caption:PHOTO(S): 1. A biology class at isCLA is so crowded that many of its 368 students line up outside a half-hour before class in hope for getting a seat. (DMN: Cindy Yamanaka). 2. From left: Cathi Early of San Francisco and Rob Early and Julia Benjamin of Los Angeles are serenaded by Harry Perry in eclectic Venice. (DMN: Cindy Yamanaka). 3. A man strolling through Los Angeles' Chinatown symbolizes growing minority presence in California and the nation. Texas is among the states expected to most sharply reflect this growing ethnic diversity. (DMN: Cindy Yamanaka). GRAPH(S): 1. Contrasting visions. 2. California population explosion. (DMN); PHOTO LOCATION: 1. - 2. NR. 3. NR(C).
TARGET ARTICLE END
